text: which van Gennep had himself pinned down in his critiques. Durkheim saw ‘individualism’ as both an epistemological and a methodological ‘enemy’ to combat, always arguing for the primacy of society. Van Gennep always insisted that individuals make choices and affect social situations in any kind of society, and this even so among the Australian tribes invoked by Durkheim. Van Gennep had made this a very direct critique of Durkheim already in the preface to his 1906 publication on Australian religion: ‘In reality, just as with us, in the Australian tribes it is the individual who invents and proposes modifications’ (van Gennep, here as quoted in Zumwalt 1982: 5). And this is something that anthropologists know quite well from their fieldworks: people, real human beings, even in the smallest and least differentiated settings, can have indeed very strong and distinct personalities. If van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim on this point had been given more resonance, we could have avoided a whole century of social theoretical attempts to posit ‘primitive society’ as an entity without individuality and therefore as an ‘opposite’ to modern human beings. In the first chapter of EFRL, Durkheim pushed forward his argument by a series of dichotomies: all religious beliefs can be placed in two categories, namely the profane and the sacred. But as we know, at the end of the day, the realm of the sacred is for Durkheim nothing but an expression of the profane in need of an ‘object’ to worship. Van Gennep likewise singled out the sacred/profane distinction as important, but as he makes clear in the third sentence of the book, the very stark dichotomy between sacred and profane is indeed a product of post-Renaissance modernity (1960: 1), and not at all present in the same way in ‘semi-civilized’ Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 59 peoples, where the sacred is part of every act and thought (ibid.: 3). Van Gennep proceeded to ground the similarities in ceremonies in the very fact of transition. Transitions from group to group or from one social situation to the next are a ‘fact of existence’ (ibid.). ‘The universe itself is governed by a periodicity which has repercussions on human life, with stages and transitions, movements forward, and periods of relative inactivity’, said van Gennep (ibid.). Much more can be made of the comparison between Durkheim and van Gennep, but for the purposes of our current argument we can stop here: Durkheim established a framework of analysis positing ritual as a timeless consolidation of society, whereas van Gennep had proposed a more open-ended framework of analysis focusing on patterns, and positing transition as the central ‘fact of life’. The point of departure for Van Gennep’s approach was constituted by real human experiences, living facts, and moments of transition, in contrast to Durkheim’s social facts, which became ‘facts’ exactly to the extent that they were external to the individual. Durkheim, said van Gennep, was pressing ethnographic data into a prefabricated and alien theoretical framework. It is a kind of phlogiston alchemy legitimized by an apparently objective scientific procedure by which things that do not fit or belong together are simply forced to fit in a ‘string of dogmatic formulas constructed without regard to the facts’ (as van Gennep would restate it in his 1920 publication on totemism; as quoted in Belmont 1979: 29). These two very different points of departure have very different potential theoretical ramifications. A recovery of van Gennep’s perspective is therefore also of relevance to contemporary theorizing. Arnold van Gennep and Marcel Mauss: So Close and yet so Far Away But how does Marcel Mauss fit in to this whole discussion? A fuller answer to this question lies outside the limits of our discussion, but a few observations must be made as they concern the general reception (and dismissal) of Arnold van Gennep’s work among both anthropologists and sociologists. Moreover, they also concern something that goes against established wisdom, but that has already been hinted at: that Marcel Mauss, despite everything, on many accounts moved sociology closer to the viewpoint of Arnold van Gennep than has so far been recognized. Van Gennep’s relationship to Durkheim and his students was tight and complex, before it suffered a final split. As we saw in Chapter 1, van Gennep had studied sciences religieuses under Léon Marillier with Marcel Mauss. Van Gennep was clearly a promising scholar, someone potentially very close to the Durkheimians. In Rites of Passage, as in other works in that period, van Gennep constructively built on the writings by Mauss, Henri Hubert and Robert Hertz. Mauss certainly knew all of van Gennep’s work. He wrote a slightly critical review of Tabou et Totémisme, but van Gennep reacted very positively to the critique, and thanked Mauss for having signalled how his interpretation could be improved (Zumwalt 1982: 4). Liminality and the Modern 60 More importantly for the present argument, Mauss wrote a short and highly critical review of Rites de Passage in L’année Sociologique (Mauss, 1910; see also Belmont’s discussion, 1979: 62–3). This review is often used as testimony to the fact that Mauss and the Durkheimians did not consider van Gennep’s work important or worthy of much consideration, and allegedly demonstrates that it was van Gennep’s own academic shortcomings and lack of theoretical rigour that prevented his entry into French academia – much more so than any conspiracy against him. Mauss’ critique therefore merits our attention. Mauss’ Review of Rites of Passage Mauss accuses van Gennep on three accounts. First, Mauss says that van Gennep sees rites of passage everywhere, and that he forces this ‘law’ of ritual passages into a principle that governs all religious representations, underpinning the very structure of thought and philosophy, ‘from the Greeks to Nietzsche’. Second, (and here the preceding argument is turned on its head), Mauss says that in a sense van Gennep is of course right about what he is saying (people use rites of passage to demarcate transitions), only that, unfortunately for van Gennep, this was already
